turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Kavanaugh has the opinion of the Court this morning in case 23-975, Seven County Infrastructure Coalition versus Eagle County, Colorado.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAVANAUGH
turns.text: In 1970, Congress passed and President Nixon signed the National Environmental Policy Act, known as NEPA. NEPA applies to certain infrastructure projects that are built, funded, or approved by the federal government, say a new highway or bridge or stadium or airport, for example. This case involves a proposed new railroad line in rural Utah. Before such a major infrastructure project can go forward, NEPA requires federal agencies to first prepare an environmental impact statement, known as an EIS. The EIS is, in essence, a report, and that report must address the significant environmental effects of a proposed project and identify feasible alternatives that could mitigate those effects. NEPA was the first of several landmark environmental laws passed by Congress in the 1970s. Subsequent statutes included the Clean Air Act Amendments of 1970, the Clean Water Act of 1972, and the Endangered Species Act of 1973, among others. Unlike those later enacted laws, however, NEPA imposes no substantive environmental obligations or restrictions. Importantly, NEPA is a purely procedural statute. It simply requires an agency to prepare an EIS report before a major project goes forward. That report, the EIS, need not weigh environmental consequences in any particular way. Rather, an agency may weigh environmental consequences as the agency reasonably sees fit under its governing statute and any relevant substantive environmental laws. So NEPA is a procedural cross-check, not a substantive roadblock to any major new projects. The goal of the law is simply to inform agency decision making, not to paralyze it. Or at least that was the idea. In this case, a Federal agency, the U.S. Surface Transportation Board, considered a proposal by a group of seven Utah counties for the construction and operation of an 88-mile railroad line in northeastern Utah. Under federal law, the Surface Transportation Board determines whether to approve construction of new railroad lines. The railroad line here would connect Utah’s oil rich Uinta Basin, a rural territory roughly the size of the State of Maryland, to the national rail network. By doing so, the new railroad line would facilitate the transportation of crude oil from Utah, ultimately to refineries in Louisiana, Texas, and elsewhere. And the project would bring significant economic development and jobs to the isolated Uinta Basin by better connecting the basin to the national economy. For that proposed 88-mile Utah railroad line, the Surface Transportation Board prepared an extraordinarily lengthy EIS, more than 3,600 pages. The Board’s EIS fully addressed the environmental effects of the proposed railroad line, but the U.S. Court of Appeals for the D.C. Circuit nonetheless faulted the Board for not sufficiently considering the environmental effects of projects separate from the construction and operation of the railroad line itself, primarily the environmental effects that could ensue from increased oil drilling in the Uinta Basin and increased oil refining along the Gulf Coast of Louisiana and Texas. On that basis, the Court of Appeals disapproved the Surface Transportation Board’s EIS, and the Court of Appeals therefore rejected the Board’s decision to approve the 88-mile railroad line. As a result, construction of the railroad line still has not begun, even though the Board approved the project back in December 2021. We now reverse the Court of Appeals for two reasons. First, the Court of Appeals did not afford the Board the substantial judicial deference required in NEPA cases. When assessing significant environmental effects and feasible alternatives for purposes of preparing an EIS under NEPA, an agency will invariably make a series of fact-dependent, context-specific, and policy-laden choices about the depth and breadth of its inquiry, and also about the length, content, and level of detail of the resulting EIS. For their part, courts should afford substantial deference and should not micromanage those agency choices so long as they fall within a broad zone of reasonableness. As this Court’s cases have previously emphasized on several occasions, and we underscore again today, inherent in NEPA is a rule of reason which ensures that agencies determine whether and to what extent to prepare an EIS based on the usefulness of any new potential information to the decision-making process. Over the years and decades, however, some courts have instead engaged in overly intrusive judicial review in NEPA cases. These rulings have slowed down or blocked many infrastructure projects and, in turn, caused litigation-averse agencies to take ever more time and to prepare ever longer EIS’s. All of that has led to more agency analysis of separate projects, more consideration of attenuated effects, more exploration of alternatives, more speculation and consultation and estimation and litigation. Delay upon delay, so much so that to borrow Justice Rehnquist’s words from one of our prior cases, the process sometimes seems to border on the Kafkaesque; fewer infrastructure projects make it to the finish line, indeed, fewer projects make it to the starting line. Those that survive often end up costing much more than is anticipated or necessary, both for the agency preparing the EIS and for the builder of the project. And that in turn means fewer and more expensive railroads, airports, highways, bridges, stadiums, and the like. And that also means fewer jobs, as new projects become difficult to finance and build in a timely fashion. A course correction of sorts is appropriate to bring judicial review under NEPA back in line with the statutory text and common sense. Congress did not design NEPA for judges to hamstring new infrastructure and construction projects. As this Court has said before, the role of a court in reviewing the sufficiency of an agency’s consideration of environmental factors is limited one. The proper principle of judicial review in NEPA cases, therefore, can be succinctly summarized deference to the agency. Second, in addition to failing to afford sufficient deference to the Surface Transportation Board, the Court of Appeals decision in this case was mistaken on the merits. The Court of Appeals erroneously required the Board to address environmental effects from projects that are separate in time or place from the 88-mile railroad project at hand, that is, effects from potential future projects or from geographically separate projects. Contrary to the Court of Appeals opinion, the EIS did not need to address the environmental consequences of future oil drilling in the basin. The potential oil drilling is distinct from construction and operation of the railroad line. Likewise, the EIS did not need to address environmental effects from increased activity at highly regulated oil refineries along the Gulf Coast of Texas and Louisiana. Both of those projects are well outside the scope of the 88-mile railroad project in rural Utah. For those reasons and others explained in our opinion, we reverse the judgment of the Court of Appeals. Justice Sotomayor has filed an opinion concurring in the judgment, which Justice Kagan and Justice Jackson have joined. Justice Gorsuch took no part in the consideration or decision of this case.
